title: Every Centiloid, from Everywhere, All at Once
authors: Ganna Blazhenets; H. Snyder; L. Apostolova; B. Asken; A. Bejanin; S. Bombois; P. Bourgeat; M. Braskie; Maria C. Carrillo; K. Casaletto; David M. Cash; Chiung-Chih Chang; Hsin-I Chang; Yishu Chao; G. Chételat; W. Coath; L. Collij; L. Colmant; B. Dickerson; Bruno Dubois; Alfonso Fajardo Valdez; G. Farrar; J. Fortea; G. Frisoni; Raquel C. Jagust; Takashi Kato; Robert Koeppe; Joel H Kramer; Susan M. Landau; B. Landeau; Sangwon Lee; B. Lopresti; V. Lowe; Xiaoxie Mao; Andrew March; C. Masters; F. Mézenge; Elizabeth C. Mormino; Maria Franquesa‐Mullerat; A. Nakamura; S. O'Bryant; Ioannis Pappas; D. Peretti; L. Quenon; C. Rowe; G. Salvadó; Jonathan M. Schott; Daniel Schwartz; C. Schwarz; S. Seo; M. Shekari; Lisa C. Silbert; Ruben Smith; Andrzej Sokołowski; David N Soleimani-Meigooni; R. Sperling; Pan Sun; A. Toga; D. E. Vaillancourt; E. M. van de Giessen; W. M. van der Flier; P. Vemuri; N. Villain; V. Villemagne; Sylvia Villeneuve; Wei‐en Wang; Michael S. W. Weiner; Cally Xiao; Fang Xie; Yeo-ri Yoon; Christina B. Young; M. Yun; G. Rabinovici; R. Joie
abstract: Ten years after the original publication, the Centiloid framework is now broadly used to harmonize amyloid‐PET quantification, facilitate data sharing and comparison across cohorts, and even assist visual interpretation in clinical settings. We evaluated the global implementation of Centiloids by comparing their distribution and corresponding positivity thresholds across cohorts.
